---
title: "The Workshop"
subtitle: "How the 90 minutes works."
description: "The run of show, the shape of each challenge, and the four prompting moves that do most of the work."
---

<svg viewBox="0 0 800 220" width="100%" style="max-height:220px;display:block;margin:0 0 1.2rem;border-radius:16px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#f6f8fc,#eef3fb);border:1px solid #e2e8f4">
  <defs>
    <linearGradient id="g-overview" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="0">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#0891b2"/>
      <stop offset="50%" stop-color="#e11d6b"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#7c3aed"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>

  <!-- section title -->
  <text x="70" y="48" fill="#475569" font-size="15" font-weight="600" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" letter-spacing="0.04em">90-MINUTE RUN OF SHOW</text>
  <text x="730" y="48" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="13" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="end">0:00 — 1:30</text>

  <!-- timeline ribbon -->
  <line x1="70" y1="115" x2="730" y2="115" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="115" x2="730" y2="115" stroke="url(#g-overview)" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>

  <!-- minute ticks -->
  <g stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1.5">
    <line x1="180" y1="108" x2="180" y2="122"/>
    <line x1="290" y1="108" x2="290" y2="122"/>
    <line x1="510" y1="108" x2="510" y2="122"/>
    <line x1="620" y1="108" x2="620" y2="122"/>
  </g>

  <!-- milestone: Intro -->
  <g>
    <circle cx="70" cy="115" r="9" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#0891b2" stroke-width="3"/>
    <circle cx="70" cy="115" r="3.5" fill="#0891b2"/>
    <text x="70" y="152" fill="#475569" font-size="13" font-weight="600" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Intro</text>
    <text x="70" y="170" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="11" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">0:00</text>
  </g>

  <!-- milestone: Challenge Match 1 -->
  <g>
    <circle cx="290" cy="115" r="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#e11d6b" stroke-width="3"/>
    <circle cx="290" cy="115" r="4" fill="#e11d6b"/>
    <text x="290" y="90" fill="#475569" font-size="13" font-weight="600" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Challenge 1</text>
    <text x="290" y="170" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="11" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">0:20</text>
  </g>

  <!-- milestone: Challenge Match 2 -->
  <g>
    <circle cx="620" cy="115" r="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="3"/>
    <circle cx="620" cy="115" r="4" fill="#7c3aed"/>
    <text x="620" y="90" fill="#475569" font-size="13" font-weight="600" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Challenge 2</text>
    <text x="620" y="170" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="11" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">0:55</text>
  </g>

  <!-- end node -->
  <g>
    <circle cx="730" cy="115" r="7" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#64748b" stroke-width="2.5"/>
    <circle cx="730" cy="115" r="2.5" fill="#64748b"/>
    <text x="730" y="152" fill="#475569" font-size="13" font-weight="600" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">Wrap</text>
    <text x="730" y="170" fill="#94a3b8" font-size="11" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">1:30</text>
  </g>

  <!-- subtle crimson signature dot -->
  <circle cx="781" cy="34" r="3" fill="#b91c1c"/>
</svg>

<div class="meta-row">
  <span class="pill cyan">90 minutes</span>
  <span class="pill rose">2 collaborative challenges</span>
  <span class="pill violet">table teams</span>
  <span class="pill amber">all experience levels</span>
</div>

<div class="box goal"><span class="box-label">Why we're here</span>
The premise of the next 90 minutes is simple: <strong>answers are cheap, judgment is expensive.</strong> Any chatbot will hand you a paragraph, a figure, or five hypotheses on demand. What it cannot hand you is the taste to know which answer is good, the skepticism to catch the one that is quietly wrong, and the framing that turns a vague wish into a useful result. That judgment is what matters most — and it is exactly the thing a working pain scientist already has. You will leave with a small, reusable toolkit of prompting moves, hands-on practice applying them to real academic tasks, and a clearer sense of where these tools earn their keep and where they fall short. Newcomers are in the right room; experienced users will still find an edge.</div>

## The 90-minute arc

| Time | Block | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| **0:00–0:10** | **Warm-up discussion (10 min)** | "What do you *actually* use AI for at work?" Round-robin at tables (not whole-room), each table posts 1 use + 1 frustration to a shared slide. Doubles as the silent **group-formation + tool-assignment** step. |
| **0:10–0:20** | **Tor: ideas & possibilities (10 min)** | The judgment-vs-answers framing; the 4 prompting moves (decompose, role, adversarial, ask-it-to-improve-your-prompt); a 60-sec live teaser of an advanced agent (Claude Code) doing something a chat app can't — *watch, don't replicate*. End by assigning each table its tool. |
| **0:20–0:55** | **Challenge 1 (35 min)** | Recommended core pick: **picture-this**. Run on the per-challenge timeline below. |
| **0:55–1:30** | **Challenge 2 (35 min)** | Recommended core pick: **plain-language-gauntlet**. Same timeline; the debrief turns to what each tool did well and where it struggled. Close the last 2 min on the **ethics moment** plus one CLI "what's possible next" pointer. |

## How each challenge works

<div class="box tip"><span class="box-label">The shape of each challenge</span>
Every 35-minute challenge follows the same three-act structure: we frame a problem, you build against it in teams, and then we compare results out loud. The instructor never hands you the method — only the goal and a deliberately weak baseline to improve on. Nobody is competing; the point is to learn from how different tables and tools approached the same task.</div>

<ul class="timeline">
  <li><span class="t">0:00–0:05 · Challenge introduction (5 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:05–0:20 · Work in your group (15 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:20–0:22 · Post your best prompt (2 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:22–0:32 · Share &amp; debrief (10 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:32–0:35 · Reset (3 min)</span></li>
</ul>

## Prompting & strategy principles

Four moves do most of the work. Hand each group a card with one to "specialize" in, then share across the room in debrief.

<div class="box goal"><span class="box-label">1 · Decompose the goal</span>
AI executes; <em>you</em> set direction. Before prompting, name the high-level goal, then the sub-tasks and angles that get you there. "Make a figure" is a wish; "show the group × time interaction, with CI error bars, a colorblind-safe palette, and a one-line takeaway caption" is a plan. <span class="pill cyan">ask: what would 'good' require?</span></div>

<div class="box good"><span class="box-label">2 · Identity / role prompting</span>
Give the agent a persona. "You are a data-viz editor for <em>Nature</em> who hates chartjunk" or "You are an expert open-source developer." A role front-loads taste, standards, and vocabulary you'd otherwise have to specify line by line. <span class="pill violet">role first, task second</span></div>

<div class="box bad"><span class="box-label">3 · Adversarial self-evaluation</span>
Make the model its own harshest reviewer. "Score your confidence 0–100 and list the three weakest claims." "Find the jargon a 10th grader would miss." "Where might this hallucinate — flag anything you can't cite." This turns a single answer into a checkable draft. <span class="pill rose">trust, then verify</span></div>

<div class="box tip"><span class="box-label">4 · Ask it to improve your prompt</span>
The cheapest upgrade: "Rewrite my prompt to get a better result, and tell me what you changed and why." Great equalizer for novices — the model teaches prompting in real time. <span class="pill teal">meta-prompting</span></div>

<div class="box ethics"><span class="box-label">The throughline</span>
Answers are cheap; judgment is expensive. Every move above moves effort from <em>producing</em> an answer to <em>auditing</em> one. That's the skill we're actually building.</div>

When you are ready, meet your tools in the [AI Toolkit](../intro/tools.qmd), or browse the full [Challenge menu](../challenges/index.qmd).
